chain_of_thought: Top native frame below abort/stack_chk_fail is Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decodeFile in libQREngine.so; Ghidra decompilation is available.
The function takes JNIEnv* (param_1), jclass/jobject (param_2, unused), and jstring (param_3). It immediately checks param_3 != 0, so NULL jstring is handled by returning 0, no crash.

It then calls through JNIEnv function table: (*env)->GetStringLength(env, jstring) via vtable offset 0x540, and GetStringChars or GetStringUTFChars via 0x548, storing result in __src. It then does `strcpy(acStack_158, __src);` where acStack_158 is a local char[256].

Length iVar3 holds the string length from JNI. There is no check that iVar3 < sizeof(acStack_158). So if the path string exceeds 255 chars, strcpy will overflow acStack_158. The compiler has inserted a stack canary (local_58 / tpidr_el0 and __stack_chk_fail), so the overflow manifests as stack canary corruption and abort -> __stack_chk_fail, which matches stack trace.

The source of the string is a Java String path passed to android.graphics.BitmapFactory.decodeFile, which in turn reaches this native method via ob.z2.J1(String). The actual path value is attacker-influenced to the extent that any file path or URL can be constructed from user input, network, or IPC in the Java layer; there is no reason to assume the app constrains filename length to < 256, and such validation is not visible in the native code. Fuzz harness likely passes long random strings, but this is also reachable in production with a long path.

After the unsafe strcpy, the code uses that buffer as file path in MsDecodeFile(acStack_158,4,&local_1180); the overflow itself is already a memory-safety bug regardless of later usage. The crash arises before or at DecodeQR when the stack canary is checked on function exit, not because of MsDecodeFile itself.

This is a classic stack-based buffer overflow (CWE-121/CWE-787). It is clearly exploitable in principle (can overwrite stack variables, saved registers, canary, etc.); stack protector only turns it into abort on detected corruption, but that is a mitigation, not a proof of non-exploitability, and other builds/architectures could be more permissive. Even with canaries, this is at least a robust DoS and likely exploitable with info-leaks or under different compile flags.

The JNI environment is realistic: Java code calls decodeFile on arbitrary paths. No strong validation chain is shown in Java callgraph; multiple activities and SDK classes call ob.z2.J1(String) which eventually calls BitmapFactory.decodeFile. User or remote-content-derived paths are realistic, so this is not a harness-only artifact.
Therefore classify as a genuine vulnerability, high severity, with high confidence.
reasons: Stack-based buffer overflow: strcpy into 256-byte local buffer from potentially longer Java string without bounds check
Crash manifested as __stack_chk_fail after stack canary corruption, consistent with overflow in Java_com_intsig_nativelib_QREngine_decodeFile
Input (file path String) is realistically attacker-controlled via Java, no validation limiting length before JNI

evidence.note: Copies unbounded Java string into 256-byte stack buffer; iVar3 is string length but only used afterward, not to bound copy.
Presence of stack canary and subsequent __stack_chk_fail matches observed abort trace.

recommendations: Replace strcpy with snprintf or strncpy using the actual buffer size (sizeof(acStack_158)) and ensure truncation handling for long paths
Optionally allocate the path buffer dynamically based on the Java string length instead of using a fixed 256-byte stack buffer
Add explicit checks on the Java string length before copying; if it exceeds the maximum safe length, reject or normalize the input
assumptions: Java side allows constructing file paths longer than 255 bytes from user or external input
MsDecodeFile and DecodeQR are not themselves causing the crash; the stack canary failure is due to the preceding strcpy overflow
limitations: Java-side source code for ob.z2.J1(String) and call sites not fully inspected; taint from user/network is inferred from typical usage patterns
Exact maximum path lengths reachable in production not confirmed, but standard Android APIs do not enforce 255-byte limits
